explanation:
**Sports training** combines science (physiology, biomechanics) and practice. Intensity zones, progressive load, recovery, and nutrition form the complete system.

## How It's Calculated

Pace per 100m based on your total time and distance.

This calculator applies the formula with your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting you to values outside reasonable limits.

## Final Notes

This calculation is a general reference. For critical decisions (training design, medical concerns, legal matters), consult with a qualified professional. Values are updated to 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.a: Pace is your time per 100m. It's the key metric swimmers use to track performance and plan training workouts.

faq.q: How do I calculate pace per 100m?
faq.a: Divide your total time (in minutes) by distance (in meters), then multiply by 100. Our calculator does this instantly.

faq.q: What's a good pace for beginners?
faq.a: Beginner swimmers typically maintain 2:00–2:30 per 100m. This is a great starting point to build endurance and technique.

faq.q: What pace do Masters swimmers achieve?
faq.a: Masters swimmers typically maintain 1:30–1:45 per 100m. This is solid recreational performance for experienced swimmers.

faq.q: What pace do elite swimmers achieve?
faq.a: Elite swimmers often swim below 1:00 per 100m in the 1500m distance. This is world-class competitive performance.

faq.q: How do I train using pace calculations?
faq.a: Use your calculated pace to structure workouts. Do repeats at your pace minus 5–10 seconds with rest between intervals.

faq.q: What's typical pace for triathlon swimming?
faq.a: Ironman triathletes typically maintain 1:40–2:00 per 100m for the 3.8km swim portion.

faq.q: How do I calculate my 1km pace?
faq.a: Multiply your 100m pace by 10. If you're 1:40/100m, expect around 16:40 for 1km.
Yes, all our calculators are free and require no registration.
